title: Permanent Marker on Cotton
timeEstimate: 15–60 minutes; honest expectation is often partial removal
stainChemistry: Permanent marker is engineered to be permanent. Xylene or toluene solvent + concentrated dye + resin binder — once the solvent evaporates, the resin-dye film is solvent-resistant by design. On cotton, the solvent carries the dye deep into the fiber before the binder sets, and the binder then locks the color in. Home chemistry has a real but limited ceiling on permanent marker: fresh marker (minutes old) often yields 70–90% to alcohol or acetone; aged / washed-in marker frequently plateaus at faint shadow no matter what. Honest expectation-setting is part of the protocol. Cotton tolerates IPA, acetone, POG, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash.
whyThisWorks: Catch cloth UNDER + solvent tamp on TOP moves dye out of the cotton into the cloths. Fresh marker responds to IPA; aged marker often needs acetone (on 100% cotton only — destroys acetate blends). Multiple passes on permanent marker are the norm. The honest framing: home treatment on permanent marker is 'fade significantly' not 'remove completely' — professionals sometimes do better, sometimes don't. Set expectations up front.
defaultAssumption: Escalate-first posture. Permanent marker on valuable clothing is often best handled by a dry cleaner while it's still fresh; home attempts that don't work delay pro treatment and make the marker harder to remove. Set expectations honestly — full removal is not guaranteed even professionally.

spottingProtocol.instruction: READ THE LABEL. If cotton is blended with acetate / triacetate, DO NOT use acetone — it dissolves those fibers. 100% cotton = acetone OK; any acetate blend = IPA or non-acetone remover only. Second fork: if the garment is valuable or the marker is older than a few hours, consider escalating to a dry cleaner first — home attempts that fail make the pro's job harder. Third fork: place a catch cloth UNDER the stain before any solvent touches it.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Isopropyl alcohol 90%+ on clean white cloth, catch cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: IPA on a clean white cloth. Tamp from the BACK of the fabric over the catch cloth. Flip cloth frequently. 3–5 passes on fresh marker. Expect the dye to come out slowly — marker resists solvent by design.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Acetone on clean white cloth — 100% cotton ONLY (destroys acetate)
spottingProtocol.instruction: If IPA plateaus and fiber is confirmed 100% cotton: acetone on a white cloth, tamp from the back. SHORT dwell — acetone evaporates fast. Flip cloth frequently. Ventilate — acetone is flammable. Never use on acetate blends or unknown blends.
Apply NSD or Dawn, soft brush. Lifts any residual resin the solvent freed. Rinse cool.
For residual dye shadow on white cotton: 3% H₂O₂. Skip on dark/dyed. One oxidizer at a time.

spottingProtocol.technique: Launder per label, accept realistic outcome
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Most home results on permanent marker are 'significantly faded, maybe partially visible.' If that's acceptable, continue to dryer. If not, escalate to a dry cleaner — and don't dryer-set first.

steps: 1. SAFETY FORK: fiber check (acetate = no acetone). Scope check (valuable / aged = consider escalation).
2. Catch cloth UNDER. IPA tamp from back. 3–5 passes.
3. Acetone on 100% cotton only if IPA plateaus. Short dwell. Ventilate.
4. NSD + Dawn to emulsify resin residue.
6. Hot wash per care label. Honest outcome expectations.
products: Acetone (100% cotton only — destroys acetate / triacetate)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: ACETONE DESTROYS ACETATE — fiber check is non-negotiable
Ventilate — acetone and IPA are flammable
Catch cloth UNDER + pressing cloth on TOP — always
Honest expectation: partial removal is the common outcome, not full removal
Dryer-set permanent marker = usually not recoverable

homeSolutions.instruction: Permanent marker (Sharpie) on cotton is genuinely hard. Before you start: (a) read the care label — if it says cotton/acetate blend or polyester/acetate blend, acetone-based removers will DISSOLVE the fiber. Use rubbing alcohol or non-acetone remover only. (b) If this is a valuable garment, consider taking it to a dry cleaner BEFORE you try home remedies — they have stronger options and home attempts that don't work make the marker harder to remove. (c) Set realistic expectations: home treatment typically FADES permanent marker significantly but rarely removes it completely.

homeSolutions.agent: Place a White Towel UNDER the Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Fold a clean white towel and place it UNDER the stained area. The dissolved ink transfers DOWN into the catch cloth, not sideways.

homeSolutions.agent: Rubbing Alcohol on a White Cloth — Tamp From the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab 70%+ rubbing alcohol on a clean white cloth. Press onto the stain from the BACK of the fabric, over the catch cloth. Flip the pressing cloth frequently. The dye transfers onto whichever cloth is touching it. Expect 3–5 rounds. Cotton tolerates alcohol.
If you don't have rubbing alcohol: alcohol-based hand sanitizer works (it's mostly alcohol + gel). Apply to a cotton ball or cloth, tamp from behind.

homeSolutions.agent: Acetone-Based Nail Polish Remover (100% Cotton Only)
homeSolutions.instruction: If alcohol plateaus AND your label confirms 100% cotton (NOT a blend): dab acetone-based nail polish remover on a cotton swab. Tamp from the back for 15–30 seconds per pass. Ventilate the room — acetone is flammable and the fumes are strong. ONE MORE TIME: if the fabric contains acetate or triacetate, acetone dissolves it. When in doubt, skip this step.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Acetone is flammable. Open a window. No smoking, no candles, no flames nearby.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Warm Water to Clean Up
homeSolutions.instruction: Once you've lifted as much as you can with alcohol / acetone, work a drop of Dawn into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Rinse cool. Dawn removes the residual resin and any solvent residue.
For a lingering color shadow on white cotton: dab 3% hydrogen peroxide, let bubble 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark / dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia.

homeSolutions.agent: Inspect and Decide — Accept or Escalate
homeSolutions.instruction: Inspect in daylight BEFORE the dryer. Three possible outcomes: (1) fully gone = hot wash per label. (2) significantly faded but still visible = decide if that's acceptable. If yes, proceed. If no, don't hot-wash/dry — take it to a dry cleaner while the treatment is still fresh. (3) barely faded or same = stop and escalate. The dry cleaner has stronger options and deserves to see the fabric before your home solvents set anything.

steps: 1. Fiber check (acetate blend? no acetone). Value check (expensive? pro first). Expectation check (partial fade is common).
3. Rubbing alcohol on a white cloth, tamp from the BACK. 3–5 rounds.
5. Acetone nail polish remover on 100% cotton ONLY if alcohol plateaus. Ventilate.
8. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Partial fade is the common outcome — decide accept vs escalate.
products: Acetone nail polish remover — 100% cotton only (NOT on acetate blends)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if acetone would damage an acetate blend, if two home rounds haven't faded the marker, if the garment is valuable and you're better off at a dry cleaner, OR if you've reached the 'significantly faded' outcome and further chemistry risks fiber damage for marginal gain.
warnings: ACETONE DESTROYS ACETATE — fiber label check first
Ventilate when using acetone or alcohol
Never pour solvent — tamp with cloth
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Set honest expectations: fade significantly is the usual outcome, full removal is rare
Never hot-wash or dry until you've accepted the current state — dryer permanently sets residual marker

neverDo: NEVER acetone on acetate / triacetate blends — dissolves fiber
Never work near open flame — acetone and alcohol are flammable
Never pour solvent on cotton — tamp with cloth
Never hot-dry residual marker — permanent set
Never over-promise removal — set honest expectations
fiberSensitivities: 100% cotton: tolerates acetone, IPA, POG, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash
Cotton / acetate blends: acetone is forbidden — non-acetone remover or IPA only
Dyed cotton: skip peroxide or inside-seam-test

materialWarnings: Home outcome is fade, not removal — set expectations
Dryer-set permanent marker = usually not recoverable

escalation.when: Acetone needed but fiber is an acetate blend, garment is valuable, two home rounds haven't faded the marker, or fade is insufficient and user needs better. Escalate EARLIER rather than later — home attempts that don't work can set the marker harder.
escalation.whatToTell: "Permanent marker on cotton — [100% cotton / blend / unknown], age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with [rubbing alcohol / acetone / both]. [Significantly faded / barely changed]. Fabric [intact / possible acetate damage]. No hot wash or dryer applied."

contextQuestions: Is the fabric 100% cotton or a blend? (blend possibly with acetate?)
Is this a valuable / sentimental item?
Has any solvent been applied already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Permanent marker is the stain where expectation-setting is half the protocol. Home treatment fades; it rarely removes. Acetate check is a fiber-safety must. Valuable items go pro earlier.
